title: Fitting a bibcock garden tap on a copper supply pipe
subtitle: Tee off a cold mains supply pipe to add a garden tap with an isolation valve and check valve
excerpt: How to fit a garden tap (bibcock) by teeing off an accessible cold supply pipe inside the house. Covers adding the tee and isolating valve, routing the pipe through the outside wall, fitting the check valve, and finishing with an external pipe connector.
sourceNotes: Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 (England and Wales). BS 6700: Design, installation, testing, and maintenance of water services. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal-inlet tap with a threaded nozzle, designed to fix to a wall or backplate and connect to a supply pipe. The inlet is threaded (typically BSP — British Standard Pipe thread): 1/2 BSP for domestic garden taps. A bibcock runs water through to a flexible hose connector. Bib taps are sold with a screwback plate that bolts against the wall.

A valve fitted on the supply side of a garden tap that prevents water from back-siphoning into the mains supply. Required by the Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 on any garden tap where a hose could be submerged. Sold as a double-check valve assembly that fits inline on the supply pipe inside the building.

content.text: requires teeing off a 15 mm cold supply pipe inside the building, routing a pipe through the external wall, and fitting the tap on a backplate on the outside. A

is required by the Water Fittings Regulations. It stops hose-contaminated water from being drawn back into the mains if pressure drops. An isolating valve inside the building lets you drain the tap each winter.

A bibcock (garden tap) with wall plate. A double-check valve assembly (15 mm compression or push-fit). A 15 mm compression tee. An inline isolating valve (quarter-turn). 15 mm copper pipe for the supply run. A 22 to 25 mm core drill bit for the wall penetration. Backplate connector for the external end. PTFE tape. Silicone sealant for the wall penetration. Pipe lagging for any external sections.
Step 1, turn off and drain the supply

Turn off the cold mains supply at the stopcock and open a tap downstream to drain the pipe. Identify the cold supply pipe to tee into; it must be the mains cold supply, not a gravity feed from the cold water tank. Cut into the supply pipe with the pipe cutter at the planned tee position and fit the compression tee. Fit the isolating valve in the spur from the tee, then fit the double-check valve downstream of the isolating valve.
Step 2, drill through the external wall

Drill a 22 to 25 mm hole through the external wall at the planned tap position; the hole must slope very slightly downward toward the outside to prevent water tracking back into the wall cavity. Use the SDS drill with a long core bit for a solid brick wall. Feed the copper pipe through the hole, leaving enough exposed outside for the backplate connection. Clip the pipe to the wall inside at 500 mm centres.
Step 3, fit the external backplate and tap

Fix the external wall plate backplate to the outside wall using masonry fixings. Connect the copper pipe stub to the backplate connector with a compression fitting. Wind two full turns of PTFE tape clockwise onto the external thread of the backplate connector. Screw the bibcock onto the backplate thread, hand-tighten, then tighten a further half-turn with the adjustable spanner; do not overtighten as the body is usually gunmetal or brass and threads strip easily. Seal around the pipe entry into the wall with exterior silicone sealant.

Restore the mains supply and check all joints for leaks. Test the tap function. At the end of each growing season, close the internal isolating valve, open the garden tap, and allow the pipe between the valve and the tap to drain fully. This prevents the external pipe section freezing and splitting in hard frost.

attrs.symptom: Compression tee joint weeping after restoring supply
attrs.cause: Olive not fully tightened against the pipe, or olive seated on a burr from a rough pipe cut.
attrs.fix: Isolate and drain. Remove the nut, check the olive is seated squarely, deburr the pipe cut if needed, and re-tighten a further quarter-turn.

attrs.symptom: Water trickling back into the building around the pipe hole through the wall
attrs.cause: Wall penetration not sealed, or the pipe hole slopes inward rather than outward.
attrs.fix: Apply exterior silicone sealant generously around the pipe at the external face. If the hole slopes inward, pack the gap at the internal face with expanding foam and seal the external face.
